The plan states the file is 'introduced by Finding 4' — a false premise; the file exists today and need only be extended, not created
findings.finding: `test_release_workflow_pins.py` already exists in the repo (introduced by story 08a8), but the plan's AC4 and What section assert it is 'introduced by Finding 4 (available since this story depends_on F4)' — implying this story must wait for F4 to create the file before it can extend check (f).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the provenance statement: `test_release_workflow_pins.py` already exists (introduced by story 08a8, not Finding 4); update the What section and AC4/AC5 to reflect that this story extends an already-present file, and re-evaluate whether `depends_on F4` is actually required for this edit.
evidence: tests/unit/test_release_workflow_pins.py exists in the repo right now, with content introduced by story 08a8

If F4 does not actually modify test_release_workflow_pins.py (since it already exists), the depends_on claim creates a phantom ordering constraint.

findings.finding: The plan's scope bullet states 'add src/rebar/_build_info.py to build_data["force_include"] so the generated (git-ignored) file is shipped in the sdist as well as the wheel,' treating this as new work. But the current hatch_build.py line 51 already runs `build_data.setdefault("force_include", {})[str(target)] = str(_TARGET)` unconditionally for any builder — including the sdist builder. The force_include for the sdist is already operative in the existing code, not missing. The plan's premise that this is new work to add may mislead the implementor into a no-op edit or a duplicate/conflicting change.
findings.location: Scope section, hatch_build.py bullet: 'add src/rebar/_build_info.py to build_data["force_include"] so the generated (git-ignored) file is shipped in the sdist'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'add src/rebar/_build_info.py to build_data["force_include"]' with a verification note that the force_include already fires for sdist builds, and focus the scope bullet on the preserve-existing logic in `initialize` as the real new addition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the What section's 'Complete the F4 evidence handshake' bullet (or add a dedicated 'Static workflow verifiability' bullet) to explicitly describe checks (g), (h), and (i) that `test_release_workflow_pins.py` must gain — matching the three assertions already enumerated in AC 5 — so the body and AC are consistent.
evidence: What section: 'this story extends the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` test that Finding 4 introduces ... adding to its check (f) an assertion that the `evidence-artifacts` fragment name + its wiring appear in `release.yml`'

AC 5: 'test_release_workflow_pins.py gains: (g) the `publish` job contains a `sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS` step AND does not rebuild...; (h) a wheel-test job and an sdist-test job exist, each `needs:` the build-once job, and each contains the named probe commands from AC 2...; (i) the build-once job produces a single named bundle...'
ABSENCE: the What section's 'Complete the F4 evidence handshake' bullet does not mention (g), (h), or (i); the Scope section does not mention them either
findings.finding: The What section says `test_release_workflow_pins.py` gets only check (f) added ('adding to its check (f) an assertion that the `evidence-artifacts` fragment name + its wiring appear in `release.yml`'), but AC 5 says the same file gains three additional checks — (g) publish job has `sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS` and no rebuild, (h) wheel-test and sdist-test jobs exist with the correct probe commands and `needs:` wiring, and (i) the build-once job produces a single named bundle. An implementer reading the body description would implement only check (f); reading AC 5 they would implement (f)+(g)+(h)+(i). The body never describes the (g)/(h)/(i) assertions.
findings.location: What section ('Complete the F4 evidence handshake' bullet) vs AC 5
scenarios: An implementer reads the What section for implementation guidance and adds only check (f) to `test_release_workflow_pins.py`, missing the three static workflow assertions that make ACs 1 and 2 in-session-verifiable — those ACs then have no test coverage.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, extend the 'Complete the F4 evidence handshake' bullet to read: '...adding to its check (f) an assertion that the `evidence-artifacts` fragment name + its wiring appear in `release.yml`; **(g)** the `publish` job contains `sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS` and no `python -m build` invocation; **(h)** both a wheel-test job and an sdist-test job exist, each with the correct `needs:` and the named probe commands from AC 2; **(i)** the build-once job produces a single named bundle containing wheel, sdist, and `SHA256SUMS`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that `consolidate_evidence`'s `needs:` list must be updated to include the new build-once job so GitHub Actions waits for the evidence-artifacts fragment before concatenating fragments.
evidence: Plan: 'the build-once job uploads an evidence-artifacts fragment (wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums) that F4's consolidation step concatenates into release-evidence.txt'
Plan: 'F4's consolidation step ... will pick it up automatically'
release.yml line 288: 'needs: [evidence_shapin, build, mcp_verify]' — the new build-once job is absent
GitHub Actions: a job does not wait for artifact producers unless they appear in its `needs:` graph
findings.finding: The plan says the `evidence-artifacts` fragment is 'picked up automatically' by `consolidate_evidence`'s `evidence-*` glob, but `consolidate_evidence`'s `needs:` list in `release.yml` currently enumerates `[evidence_shapin, build, mcp_verify]`. If the new build-once job is not added to that `needs:` list, GitHub Actions will schedule `consolidate_evidence` without waiting for the build-once job to finish uploading the fragment — the glob will run before the artifact exists and the fragment will be silently missing from `release-evidence.txt`.
findings.location: What section ('Complete the F4 evidence handshake') / release.yml: consolidate_evidence job (lines 283-305)
scenarios: The consolidate_evidence job starts before the build-once job uploads evidence-artifacts; the download-artifact step either fails or downloads an empty set, silently omitting the wheel/sdist SHAs from release-evidence.txt.
A reviewer inspects release-evidence.txt and finds the wheel/sdist hashes absent despite the plan promising them — the evidence handshake silently fails.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for release.yml and in AC4, add: 'update `consolidate_evidence.needs` to include the build-once job name so the evidence-* glob always finds the evidence-artifacts fragment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the Scope/What sections that `consolidate_evidence`'s `needs:` must be updated to include the new build-once job, so the `evidence-artifacts` fragment is available before consolidation runs.
evidence: release.yml line 288: `needs: [evidence_shapin, build, mcp_verify]` — does not include any build-once job
release.yml line 291-294: `uses: actions/download-artifact` with `pattern: evidence-*` — the glob picks up artifacts from already-completed jobs, but job completion is gated by `needs:`
The plan's What section says the glob 'will pick it up automatically' without noting the `needs:` update required
findings.finding: The plan asserts the `evidence-*` glob in `consolidate_evidence` 'will pick up [the new `evidence-artifacts` fragment] automatically' — but the job's `needs:` currently lists only `[evidence_shapin, build, mcp_verify]`. Adding a new job that produces `evidence-artifacts` does NOT automatically make that artifact available unless the new job is also added to `needs:`. Without the new build-once job in `needs:`, `consolidate_evidence` may run before the artifact is uploaded, causing a race or missing fragment.
findings.location: What section: 'the build-once job uploads an `evidence-artifacts` fragment (the wheel/sdist SHA-256 sums) that F4's consolidation step concatenates into `release-evidence.txt`, and this story extends the `test_release_workflow_pins.py` test'; release.yml line 286-288: `needs: [evidence_shapin, build, mcp_verify]`
scenarios: The build-once job produces `evidence-artifacts` but `consolidate_evidence` runs before it completes (because the new job isn't in `needs:`), so the fragment is missing from `release-evidence.txt`.
GitHub Actions artifact availability depends on job completion ordering (via `needs:`), not just the glob pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Add the new build-once job name to `consolidate_evidence`'s `needs:` list alongside `evidence_shapin`, `build`, and `mcp_verify`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `test.yml` bullet in Scope: either commit to it (add a corresponding AC) or explicitly mark it out-of-scope for this story (e.g. 'deferred to a follow-on ticket') so the executor has no decision to make.
evidence: 'optionally add a wheel/sdist artifact probe to regular CI so the defect is caught pre-release, not only in the release run'
No AC references test.yml changes; every AC maps to release.yml, hatch_build.py, test_attestation_signing.py, or docs/releasing.md
findings.finding: The Scope section marks `test.yml` changes as 'optionally add a wheel/sdist artifact probe to regular CI' without resolving whether this is in-scope or out-of-scope for this story. No AC maps to it. An executor cannot determine whether to implement it, skip it, or defer it to a follow-on ticket.
findings.location: Scope section, `.github/workflows/test.yml` bullet
scenarios: Executor implements test.yml changes, inflating scope and potentially conflicting with F1/F4 edits to the same file.
Executor skips test.yml changes, leaving the stated goal ('so the defect is caught pre-release') unmet with no record of the decision.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note: 'Out of scope for this story; tracked as a follow-on improvement.' and remove the 'optionally' qualifier — or add a concrete AC: '`test.yml` gains a nightly wheel/sdist probe job that installs the latest release wheel from PyPI and runs the same probes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named in-session unit test (e.g. `test_hook_preserves_existing_commit_when_no_git_and_no_env_var`) to `tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py` that: (a) writes a `_build_info.py` with a known non-null COMMIT into a temp tree, (b) invokes the hook's `initialize` with `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` unset and no `.git`, and (c) asserts the output `_build_info.py` still contains the original non-null COMMIT — then reference this test in AC 3.
evidence: AC 3 names tests: (a) test_hook_fails_when_rebar_build_commit_empty, (b) test_hook_falls_back_to_git_when_env_unset, plus the unnamed env-var sdist→wheel test — none of these set REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT unset AND pre-populate _build_info.py with a non-null COMMIT to verify preservation.

What section: 'Step (2) is essential for install-from-sdist: pip install <sdist> rebuilds a wheel from the extracted tree (re-running this hook) with no .git and no env var, so without preserve-existing the SHA the sdist shipped would be overwritten with None'
The sdist-test workflow job asserts non-null COMMIT but is gated behind a live release run; static YAML check (h) only verifies YAML structure, not runtime behavior of the hook.
E6(f): a cutover to a new code path (step 2) needs an in-session criterion that exercises it, not only an offline/structural proxy.
findings.finding: The preserve-existing code path (step 2 of the four-step hook precedence in hatch_build.py) — the logic that keeps an sdist-shipped non-null COMMIT when `pip install <sdist>` re-runs the hook with no `.git` and no `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` — has no specified in-session unit test. The three tests named or implied in AC 3 cover only: (1) env-var injection (REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT set), (2) fail-fast (env var set-but-empty), and (3) git fallback (env var unset, .git present). No test directly exercises step 2. The sdist-test workflow job exercises it end-to-end, but is only verifiable in a live release run; static YAML check (h) verifies the job's YAML structure, not that the preserve-existing code path actually works correctly.
findings.location: AC 3 (named tests), Scope `hatch_build.py` bullet ('preserve an existing non-null COMMIT'), What section ('Step (2) is essential for install-from-sdist')
scenarios: The preserve-existing logic is implemented incorrectly (e.g. always overwrites, or reads the wrong key) — all named tests pass, make test is green, but end-user pip install <sdist> still yields COMMIT=None.
A future refactor of _build_info.py's format silently breaks the preserve-existing read, with no test to catch it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC 3: '(c) `test_hook_preserves_existing_commit_when_no_git_and_no_env_var` — in a temp dir with a pre-populated `_build_info.py` containing `COMMIT = "abc1234"`, `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` unset, and no `.git`, invoke `initialize` (or run `pip install .` with `--no-build-isolation`) and assert the resulting installed `_build_info.COMMIT == "abc1234"` (not None).' Wire this into the `make test` suite so the preserve-existing contract is codebase-verifiable in-session.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test (e.g., `test_hook_preserves_existing_commit_when_no_git_and_env_unset`) to `tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py` that writes a `_build_info.py` with a known non-null COMMIT into a temp tree with no `.git` and `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` unset, runs the hook's initialize logic, and asserts the output `_build_info.py` still contains that COMMIT value — making the preserve-existing contract verifiable by `make test`, not only by a live CI run.
evidence: Plan states: 'Both branches are pinned by concrete tests in tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py: (a) test_hook_fails_when_rebar_build_commit_empty ... and (b) test_hook_falls_back_to_git_when_env_unset' — only two of the four steps (1 and 3) are covered; step (2) preserve-existing is absent from this list.
The preserve-existing path is the plan's most novel addition and is essential for the sdist install provenance guarantee, yet no unit test isolates it.
The sdist-test workflow job (AC 2) exercises this path end-to-end but only in a live CI run, not in make test.
Static check (h) in test_release_workflow_pins.py only verifies the sdist-test job exists with the right commands — not that the hook actually preserves the existing COMMIT value.
findings.finding: The four-step precedence logic's step (2) — preserve an existing non-null COMMIT in _build_info.py — has no unit test in tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py. The plan explicitly names unit tests for the fail-fast branch (step 1 set-but-empty) and the git-fallback branch (step 3), but step (2) is exercised only by the workflow-level sdist-test CI job. The test_release_workflow_pins.py static check (h) verifies the job structure but not the behavioral output. Running `make test` locally cannot verify that the preserve-existing step actually returns the baked COMMIT rather than falling through to None.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC 3 / tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py / hatch_build.py preserve-existing step
scenarios: A developer refactors hatch_build.py and accidentally inverts the precedence order (preserve-existing before env-var, or drops step 2 entirely) — make test passes green but sdist installs silently ship COMMIT=None.

The edge case where _build_info.py exists with COMMIT=None (null) is not tested — the plan specifies 'non-null' as the preservation condition, but no test verifies the hook falls through correctly when the existing value is null.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py:
```python
def test_hook_preserves_existing_commit_when_no_git_and_env_unset(tmp_path):
    # Write a _build_info.py with a known baked SHA into a temp tree with no .git
    build_info = tmp_path / 'src' / 'rebar' / '_build_info.py'
    build_info.parent.mkdir(parents=True)
    build_info.write_text('COMMIT = "abc1234"\n')
    env = {k: v for k, v in os.environ.items() if k != 'REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT'}
    # Run python -m build (or invoke initialize directly) in the temp tree
    # Assert the resulting _build_info.py still contains COMMIT = 'abc1234'
    ...
```
Also add a companion test `test_hook_does_not_preserve_null_commit` that writes `COMMIT = None` and asserts the hook falls through to git (or None if no git) rather than preserving the null.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the inline `r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')` probe in the What section, AC2, and AC5 check (h) with a probe that calls the existing `rebar.schemas` API — e.g. `python -c "from rebar import schemas; assert len(schemas.names()) > 0; [schemas.load(n) for n in schemas.names()]"` — so the wheel/sdist smoke test exercises the real packaging path rather than reinventing schema enumeration.
evidence: Plan prescribes: `r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')` as the schema-load probe
src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py:348-352 — `names()` lists every schema file via `files(__package__).glob('*.schema.json')`
tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_outputs.py:48,59 — `schemas.names()` called
tests/interfaces/contracts/test_schema_coverage.py:44,54 — `schemas.names()` called
src/rebar/llm/findings.py:256 — `schemas.names()` called in production
No call-site for `files('rebar').rglob('*.json')` exists in the codebase (confirmed by search)
findings.finding: The JSON-schema probe prescribed in What, AC2, and AC5 (`python -c "import importlib.resources as r, json, rebar; [json.loads(f.read_text()) for f in r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')]"`) invents a new `importlib.resources.files().rglob()` traversal on the top-level `rebar` package that is not used anywhere in the production codebase, when `rebar.schemas.names()` + `rebar.schemas.load()` already provide exactly this capability and have 4+ existing call-sites.
findings.location: What section (JSON-schema probe inline command); AC2; AC5 check (h)
scenarios: The probe silently counts non-schema JSON files (e.g. rebar.toml, server.json, any fixture .json under src/rebar) in its assertion, making the 'count non-zero' check unreliable as a schema-specific probe.

A future refactor moving schemas to a sub-package would silently break the `rglob` probe but leave the existing `schemas.names()` path intact — the probe adds fragility without adding coverage beyond what the existing abstraction already provides.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the schema probe command in What, AC2, and AC5 to: `python -c "from rebar import schemas, schemas as _s; names = _s.names(); assert names, 'no schemas'; [_s.load(n) for n in names]"`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify empirically (e.g., by inspecting Hatchling source or running a test build) that `build_data['force_include']` in the hook `initialize` is honored during sdist builds — not only wheel builds — before relying on it as the mechanism to ship `_build_info.py` in the sdist.
evidence: hatch_build.py:51 — `build_data.setdefault('force_include', {})[str(target)] = str(_TARGET)` already exists for wheel builds
pyproject.toml lines 239-252 show `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` uses only `include`/`exclude` — no `force_include` or `artifacts`
No repo code or test verifies that Hatchling honors `build_data['force_include']` during an sdist build (as opposed to a wheel build)
The plan's own parenthetical acknowledges `artifacts=` is 'silently ignored' for sdist — a related unverified Hatchling claim
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the hook's `force_include` works for both targets [wheel AND sdist]' and treats Hatchling's `build_data['force_include']` in the hook `initialize` as a supported mechanism for shipping `_build_info.py` in the sdist — but no code in the repo verifies this Hatchling behavior, and the plan simultaneously notes that `artifacts=` is silently ignored for sdist (another Hatchling behavioral claim). If `force_include` is also silently ignored for sdist builds, the preserve-existing step in the hook has nothing to preserve and the entire sdist provenance guarantee fails.
findings.location: Scope bullet: 'add `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to `build_data["force_include"]` so the generated (git-ignored) file is shipped in the **sdist** as well as the wheel. (Do NOT add `artifacts=` to the sdist pyproject target — Hatchling's sdist builder uses `include`/`exclude` only and silently ignores `artifacts`, a wheel-only key; the hook's `force_include` works for both targets.)'
scenarios: If Hatchling's sdist builder silently ignores `force_include` in `build_data` (treating it as wheel-only, like `artifacts`), the sdist will never contain `_build_info.py`, the preserve-existing step in step (2) has nothing to preserve, and `pip install <sdist>` will always produce `COMMIT = None`.
The sdist-test job in AC2 would catch this failure — but only after implementation, not during planning.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (cite Hatchling source or add a unit test) that `build_data['force_include']` in the `initialize` hook is honoured by the sdist builder, not only the wheel builder; if it is not, specify an alternative mechanism (e.g. `pyproject.toml` `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist] include` or a direct `shared-data`/`artifacts` hook key) to ensure `_build_info.py` ships in the sdist.
evidence: Plan: 'the hook's force_include works for both targets' — asserted without evidence
Plan: 'Do NOT add artifacts= to the sdist pyproject target — Hatchling's sdist builder uses include/exclude only and silently ignores artifacts'
Current hatch_build.py line 51: force_include is already set; current pyproject.toml also lists the file under wheel artifacts — but no sdist-level test exists
AC2 and What section: 'the sdist shipped _build_info.py with the SHA baked during the release build' — this depends entirely on force_include working for sdist
findings.finding: The plan asserts `build_data['force_include']` in the Hatchling `initialize` hook ships `_build_info.py` in the **sdist** as well as the wheel, but the existing hook already uses `force_include` only to ship into the wheel (the pyproject `artifacts` key is the wheel-builder's copy of the same mechanism). The plan explicitly warns that pyproject-level `artifacts` is wheel-only and says the hook's `force_include` works for both — but provides no evidence or test for the sdist case. If Hatchling's sdist builder does NOT honour `force_include` in `build_data` for the hook path, `_build_info.py` is never shipped in the sdist, the preserve-existing path (step 2) has nothing to preserve, and the entire fix for the sdist→wheel `COMMIT=None` path silently fails.
findings.location: Scope section ('hatch_build.py (initialize) — add src/rebar/_build_info.py to build_data[force_include]')
scenarios: force_include in build_data is silently ignored by Hatchling's sdist builder (as pyproject `artifacts` is); _build_info.py is absent from the sdist; pip install <sdist> rebuilds a wheel with COMMIT=None; the sdist-test job asserts non-null COMMIT and fails, exposing the gap — but only at release-run time, not in the unit test suite.

The sdist-test job in AC2 would catch this at release time, but if the sdist builder silently drops the file, the job fails in a confusing way (COMMIT=None) rather than a clear 'force_include not honoured' error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (against Hatchling's sdist builder source or docs) that `build_data['force_include']` in `initialize` is honoured when the builder target is `sdist`; if it is not, use `build_data['shared_data']` or an explicit `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist] include` entry instead, and add a test that builds an sdist in a tmp directory and checks that `_build_info.py` is present inside the tarball.
evidence: Plan states: 'the hook's `force_include` works for both targets' — asserted as fact, not verified
Existing hatch_build.py comment (line 50): 'Ensure the generated (git-ignored) file ships in the wheel.' — note 'wheel' only
pyproject.toml: `artifacts` list for `_build_info.py` lives under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]`, not under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]`

Plan also warns 'Do NOT add `artifacts=` to the sdist pyproject target — Hatchling's sdist builder uses `include`/`exclude` only and silently ignores `artifacts`' — acknowledging sdist builder behaves differently, yet asserts `force_include` is the working alternative without citing a source
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `build_data['force_include']` in Hatchling's `initialize` hook ships `_build_info.py` in the **sdist** as well as the wheel, but this is unverified against Hatchling's sdist builder. The existing `hatch_build.py` comment says only 'Ensure the generated (git-ignored) file ships in the wheel', and `pyproject.toml`'s `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel]` `artifacts` list (not `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]`) already force-includes it for the wheel. Hatchling's sdist builder may ignore `force_include` keys it does not recognize or handle them differently from the wheel builder. The entire preserve-existing chain (step 2 of the four-step precedence) depends on `_build_info.py` landing in the sdist with a baked SHA — if `force_include` is silently ignored by the sdist builder, the shipped sdist will lack the file, the preserve-existing path has nothing to preserve, and the sdist-test job's non-null COMMIT assert will fail at release time.
findings.location: Scope section — '`hatch_build.py` (`initialize`) — add `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to `build_data["force_include"]` so the generated (git-ignored) file is shipped in the **sdist** as well as the wheel.'
scenarios: Hatchling's sdist builder silently ignores `build_data['force_include']` → shipped sdist lacks `_build_info.py` → `pip install <sdist>` triggers hook with no pre-existing file, no git tree, and no env var → installs with `COMMIT = None` → the sdist-test job fails at release time, not in pre-release CI
Developer verifies locally (with `.git` present) that their sdist install works because git fallback fires, never catching that `force_include` was ineffective for the sdist
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concrete test: build an sdist in a temp dir using `hatchling.builders.sdist.SdistBuilder`, open the resulting `.tar.gz`, and assert `_build_info.py` is present with a non-null `COMMIT` — this would make the capability assertion codebase-verifiable before the preserve-existing tests can pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')` with a `Traversable`-compatible traversal (e.g. using `rebar.schemas.names()` from the existing schema registry, or a recursive `iterdir()` helper) so the probe works on both filesystem-backed and zip-backed traversables.
evidence: Plan prescribes: `python -c "import importlib.resources as r, json, rebar; [json.loads(f.read_text()) for f in r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')]"` in both What and AC2/AC5.
resolve_symbol('rglob', 'importlib.resources.abc') => UNRESOLVED — rglob is not part of the Traversable ABC interface.
ABSENCE: the codebase never calls `.rglob` on `importlib.resources.files()`; production code uses `iterdir()` (src/rebar/_cli/_help.py:62) and path joins (src/rebar/_engine.py:37).
Partially-verified: works on filesystem-backed traversable (standard wheel install on disk); unverified for zip-backed traversable.
findings.finding: The probe command `r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')` prescribed in What, AC2, and AC5 calls `.rglob()` on the object returned by `importlib.resources.files()`. The `importlib.resources.Traversable` ABC does not define `rglob` — it exposes only `iterdir()`, `open()`, `read_text()`, `read_bytes()`, `is_dir()`, `is_file()`, `joinpath()`, `__truediv__`, and `name`. The concrete `Path`-backed implementation adds `rglob`, but a zip-backed traversable (as may occur with a wheel installed in a zip-safe context) does not. The codebase's own production code never uses `.rglob` on `importlib.resources.files()` — it uses `iterdir()` or explicit path joins. If the probe runs in any environment where the rebar package is zip-extracted (e.g. zipapp, some zip-safe wheel install scenarios), the probe will raise `AttributeError`, causing a false-negative test failure unrelated to the actual provenance gap being tested. Evidence class: Partially-verified (works on filesystem install; unverified for zip-backed traversables).
findings.location: What section (inline probe command) and AC2, AC5 (named probe)
scenarios: The sdist-test job installs from a clean sdist in a temp dir — the package should be filesystem-backed, so rglob may work in practice. But the probe is prescribed as a general artifact test, and if it runs against a zip-extracted wheel the test will AttributeError rather than test provenance.
A future test environment or CI runner that installs wheels in zip-safe mode would silently break the probe, masking the actual provenance assertion.
findings.suggested_fix: Use the existing `rebar.schemas.names()` API which already enumerates all packaged JSON schemas: `python -c "import rebar.schemas as s, json; schemas = [s.load(n) for n in s.names()]; assert len(schemas) > 0"`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (against hatchling's sdist builder source or hatchling docs) that `build_data['force_include']` set in a custom hook's `initialize` is honoured by the sdist builder, and add a concrete sdist-inclusion test (analogous to `test_built_wheel_bakes_build_info` but using `SdistBuilder`) to pin this behaviour before shipping.
evidence: Plan states: 'the hook's `force_include` works for both targets' and '(Do NOT add `artifacts=` to the sdist pyproject target — Hatchling's sdist builder uses `include`/`exclude` only and silently ignores `artifacts`, a wheel-only key; the hook's `force_include` works for both targets.)'
Existing hatch_build.py:51: `build_data.setdefault('force_include', {})[str(target)] = str(_TARGET)` — only tested in the wheel context (test_built_wheel_bakes_build_info uses WheelBuilder directly).
ABSENCE: no test in the codebase exercises `force_include` via the sdist builder path.
Partially-verified: force_include works for wheel (confirmed by existing test); sdist builder behaviour is unverified against hatchling sdist source.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that adding `_build_info.py` to `build_data['force_include']` inside the Hatchling hook's `initialize` method causes the file to ship in the **sdist** as well as the wheel. The existing `hatch_build.py` already uses `force_include` in the wheel context and the current `pyproject.toml` includes `_build_info.py` under `[tool.hatch.build.targets.wheel] artifacts`. However, whether Hatchling's **sdist builder** honours `build_data['force_include']` set by a custom build hook is unverified — the sdist and wheel builders have separate `build_data` contracts and the sdist builder's treatment of `force_include` is not confirmed against hatchling's sdist source. The plan explicitly warns against using `artifacts=` for sdist (correctly noting it's wheel-only) and pins the mechanism to `force_include`, but does not cite documentation or a test confirming the sdist builder respects `force_include`. Evidence class: Partially-verified (force_include confirmed for wheel; unverified for sdist).
findings.location: Scope bullet: '`hatch_build.py` (`initialize`) — add `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to `build_data["force_include"]` so the generated (git-ignored) file is shipped in the **sdist** as well as the wheel.'
scenarios: If the sdist builder does not honour `force_include`, the generated `_build_info.py` will not be included in the sdist, the preserve-existing path (step 2) will have nothing to preserve, and the published sdist will yield `COMMIT = None` on user install — the exact defect this story aims to fix.

findings.finding: The inline JSON-schema probe command prescribed in the What section and AC2 — `python -c "import importlib.resources as r, json, rebar; [json.loads(f.read_text()) for f in r.files('rebar').rglob('*.json')]"` — calls `.rglob()` on an `importlib.resources.Traversable` object, a method that does not exist in Python 3.12. `Traversable.rglob()` (and `.glob()`) were added in Python 3.13 (CPython gh-90172). All CI and release jobs run `python-version: "3.12"`. Calling `.rglob()` on the `Traversable` returned by `files('rebar')` raises `AttributeError` at runtime, so the probe always fails on the release runner regardless of artifact correctness.
findings.location: What section — 'Test the exact wheel' probe command; AC2 — 'load every packaged JSON schema via `importlib.resources` (count non-zero, each parses)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the probe one-liner with: `python -c "import importlib.resources as r, json, rebar; pkg = r.files('rebar'); schemas = [f for f in pkg.joinpath('schemas').iterdir() if f.name.endswith('.json')]; assert schemas; [json.loads(f.read_text()) for f in schemas]; print(len(schemas), 'schemas OK')"` — this uses only the `Traversable.iterdir()` API available since Python 3.9.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief developer-facing note to `CLAUDE.md` (or a new `docs/adr/build-provenance-hook.md`) documenting the `hatch_build.py` four-step precedence (`REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` → preserve-existing → git → None), the rationale for each step, the fail-fast scope (env var set-but-empty only), and the `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` env-var interface — so the hook contract is discoverable without reading the source.
evidence: 'Fix the hook — a four-step precedence (env var + preserve-existing + git)' is a novel pattern not covered by existing docs
Scope lists docs/releasing.md as destination but only for the 'Build-once section' and rewriting 'Post-release smoke test'
No mention of CLAUDE.md or an ADR for the hook's precedence contract or env-var interface
T5e(c): 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design-doc update'
T7(a): 'A new pattern/contract/config/CLI gets a doc/ADR'
findings.finding: The four-step hook precedence (`REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` → preserve-existing → git → None), the env-var interface contract, and the build-once pipeline pattern are novel architectural decisions that affect how any future maintainer modifies `hatch_build.py` or the release workflow — but the plan scopes its documentation update only to `docs/releasing.md` (the user-facing release process) and does not mention updating `CLAUDE.md` or creating an ADR to capture the developer-facing hook contract and its rationale.
findings.location: Scope section (hatch_build.py bullet, docs/releasing.md bullet); AC 6
scenarios: A future maintainer modifies `hatch_build.py` without understanding the preserve-existing rationale and accidentally removes step (2), silently breaking sdist installs' provenance
A new contributor adds a second env-var override to the hook without knowing the precedence chain is intentional, creating ordering conflicts
The `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` interface is undiscoverable except by reading `hatch_build.py` source, making it hard to replicate in a fork or CI variant
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `## Build provenance hook` subsection to `CLAUDE.md` (under an existing 'Architecture' or 'Development' heading) with: (1) the four-step precedence chain and its rationale, (2) when `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` should be set (release builds only), (3) the preserve-existing invariant for sdist installs, and (4) the fail-fast/graceful-degradation boundary. Alternatively, create `docs/adr/0NNN-build-provenance-hook.md` if the project uses numbered ADRs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the preserve-existing read of `_build_info.py` is wrapped in a try/except (catching at minimum `SyntaxError`, `OSError`, and parse failures), falling through to step (3) git-fallback on any error — so a malformed file degrades gracefully rather than aborting the install.
evidence: 'preserve an existing non-null COMMIT already in _build_info.py if one is present' — no parse-error handling specified
The plan specifies the hook raises on REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT set-but-empty, but is silent on file-read/parse failures in step (2)
ABSENCE: no try/except or fallback specified for the _build_info.py read in the preserve-existing path
findings.finding: The preserve-existing step (step 2) reads and parses `_build_info.py` to extract the existing `COMMIT` value, but the plan specifies no error handling for a malformed, truncated, or syntactically invalid file. A corrupted `_build_info.py` shipped inside the sdist would cause the hook to throw an unhandled exception during `pip install <sdist>` (the wheel-from-sdist rebuild), making the package uninstallable from that sdist with no actionable error message.
findings.location: hatch_build.py — four-step precedence, step (2) preserve-existing; Scope section paragraph on preserve-existing; AC 2 sdist-test job
scenarios: A bit-flip or partial write leaves _build_info.py with a syntax error inside the sdist; `pip install rebar-<ver>.tar.gz` fails with an opaque ImportError or SyntaxError from inside the hook with no hint about the corrupted file
A downstream packager repackages the sdist and inadvertently truncates _build_info.py; every downstream install fails
findings.suggested_fix: In `hatch_build.py`'s step (2), use `ast.literal_eval` or a regex to extract the COMMIT value, wrapped in a broad `except Exception: pass` that falls through to step (3); document in a comment that step (2) is best-effort and a read failure is non-fatal (the env-var and git paths are the authoritative sources).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `github_release` as an explicit edit target in the Scope section and update its `download-artifact` step to use the new named bundle (matching the name chosen for the build-once artifact) rather than `name: dist`.
evidence: release.yml lines 135-138: `- uses: actions/download-artifact@... with: name: dist path: dist/`
Plan Scope: 'release.yml — build-once + two artifact-test jobs + hash-gated publish' — enumerates publish and test jobs but not github_release
Plan AC1: 'publish downloads that same bundle and runs sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS' — only publish is mentioned as consuming the new bundle name
ABSENCE: the plan never names github_release as a consumer that needs its `name: dist` download step updated to the new bundle name
findings.finding: The `github_release` job in release.yml downloads the artifact by the current name `dist` (line 135-138: `name: dist`), but the plan restructures the build job to emit a new named bundle. This job is not enumerated as a consumer requiring update in the Scope section.
findings.location: Scope section / .github/workflows/release.yml lines 135-138 (`github_release` job)
scenarios: After this story lands, the `github_release` job still downloads `name: dist`, which no longer exists (it was replaced by the new named bundle). The GitHub Release creation step fails because `dist/` is empty.

A release tag push triggers the workflow; publish succeeds (it downloads the correct new bundle), but github_release fails silently or with a 404 artifact download error, leaving no GitHub Release for the tag.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for release.yml, add: 'the `github_release` job's `download-artifact` step must be updated from `name: dist` to the new bundle name.' In the workflow, update the `github_release` download step's `name:` to match whatever the build-once job names its upload artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the PyPI publish step's access posture in the plan: state whether it uses OIDC Trusted Publishing (with scoped subject claim matching only the release workflow/tag) or a stored API token (scoped to this project only), and state whether a GitHub Actions environment with protection rules gates the `publish` job — so the security contract of the modified boundary-crossing path is explicit.
evidence: Plan modifies `release.yml` publish job: 'publish `needs:` both test jobs, downloads the original bundle, recomputes and compares hashes against the recorded build hashes, and publishes without rebuilding'
No mention of PyPI auth mechanism (OIDC vs token), no mention of GitHub Actions environment protection rules gating the publish job
The plan explicitly states it coordinates with Finding 1 and Finding 4 on `release.yml` edits, but neither this plan nor those references declare the publish-step auth posture
findings.finding: The plan restructures and modifies the `release.yml` publish job (a boundary-crossing component that authenticates to PyPI over the public internet) but never declares the access posture of the publish step — it does not state whether PyPI authentication uses OIDC Trusted Publishing with a scoped subject claim, a stored API token secret, or another mechanism, and does not declare whether a GitHub Actions environment with required-reviewers / protection rules gates the publish job. Per the 'undeclared access posture' framing, this is a gap on a modified boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Scope section (`release.yml` — publish job) and AC 1 (`publish downloads that same bundle … and publishes without rebuilding`)
scenarios: If the publish job lacks a GitHub Actions environment gate, any approved PR merge or accidental workflow trigger could publish to PyPI without a human approval checkpoint
If a stored API token is used with project-wide or account-wide scope rather than a per-project scoped token, a compromised workflow could push to unintended packages
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence note to the Scope section under `release.yml`: e.g. 'The publish job runs in the `release` GitHub Actions environment (requires maintainer approval); PyPI authentication uses OIDC Trusted Publishing with a subject claim of `repo:<org>/<repo>:ref:refs/tags/*`, scoped to this project only — no stored token is introduced or modified by this plan.' If the existing posture differs, document what it actually is.

findings.finding: ACs 1 and 2 describe release-workflow-level behaviors (sha256sum hash-gate in publish, wheel/sdist test job probes) that are only exercised when the release workflow actually fires; no in-session static test (analogous to test_release_workflow_pins.py's checks) is specified to verify that release.yml contains the hash-check step and the named probe commands.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 1 ('sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS … failing on any mismatch'), AC 2 (wheel-test and sdist-test jobs with named probes)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC 4 description: 'The same test file also gains checks (g) sha256sum -c SHA256SUMS appears in the publish job steps, and (h) wheel-test and sdist-test jobs exist with the probe commands listed in AC 2.' This makes ACs 1 and 2 in-session completable.

findings.finding: The env-var propagation test (REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=<short-hex> python -m build, assert wheel carries that SHA through the sdist→wheel path) is described in the 'What' section and referenced obliquely in AC 3, but never given a function name in the ACs — unlike the two explicitly named tests — making it impossible to verify its completeness from the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 3 ('a test … asserts the wheel built via the default sdist→wheel path contains COMMIT equal to that short SHA'); What section — 'Verify env-var propagation' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: In AC 3, after listing the two named tests, add: '(c) test_hook_uses_env_var_through_sdist_wheel_path runs REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=<7-hex-char> python -m build in a temp checkout (no .git needed since the env var is set) and asserts the resulting wheel's _build_info.py contains COMMIT == that exact value.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
